FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development Study, wave 1 release, 6758 individuals (49.4% female, mean age 9.94 years)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: latent profile analysis (LPA) with cross-validated training/testing samples identifying seven neurodevelopmental profiles based on fMRI functional activation patterns

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging, 2022

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper does not include a qualifying smartphone or social media variable as IV or DV. Screen time is mentioned only as a demographic correlate when comparing profiles ("more screen time (F = 6.80, p < .0001)"), not as a measured construct analyzed in relation to an individual-trait-level outcome

notes: Core analysis is identification of neurodevelopmental profiles via latent profile analysis. Screen time appears as one of several demographic characteristics found to differ across profiles but is not the focus of a substantive SM-outcome relationship analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, May 2020 COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release, 5412 adolescents aged 10-14 years (50.7% female; 7.2% Asian; 11.1% Black; 17.2% Hispanic/Latino; 60.6% White)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional multiple linear regression of mental health and resiliency factors (independent variables) with total screen use (dependent variable, calculated as sum across 7 modalities)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed letter in JAMA Pediatrics, January 2022, Volume 176, Number 1

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM/phone variables measured: 7 distinct screen modalities (multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing internet, streaming video/TV). These are phone/SM-specific uses. Paired with individual-trait-level variables: mental health (individual-level well-being), perceived stress (individual-level construct), social support (individual-trait), and coping behaviors (behavioral trait). Screen use is DV, psychosocial factors are IVs.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported for SM-trait associations in adjusted models (Table 2): mental health B=0.29 (95% CI 0.06-0.52, p=.01); perceived stress B=0.67 (95% CI 0.43-0.91, p<.001); social support B=-0.32 (95% CI -0.59 to -0.04, p=.02); coping behaviors B=-0.17 (95% CI -0.26 to -0.09, p<.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results present: regression coefficients (β) with 95% confidence intervals and p-values for associations between each mental health/resiliency factor and total screen use

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional, not longitudinal; EC7 does not apply
notes: Strong paper with clear SM-DV and psychosocial-IV pairing; adequate sample size and adjustment for sociodemographic confounders

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, May 2020 COVID-19 survey data collection, 5335 adolescents aged 10-14 years (12.5±0.9 years, 50.9% female)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional comparison of parent-reported vs. adolescent-reported screen time, linear regression models assessing sociodemographic predictors of reporting discrepancies

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Academic Pediatrics, April 2022, Volume 22, Number 3

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures screen time modalities (texting, video chatting, multiplayer gaming, social media, total) but does not pair smartphone/SM variables with a separate individual-trait-level outcome or predictor in a substantive analysis. The core analysis examines concordance/discrepancy between parent and adolescent REPORTS of screen time—not the association between SM use and a health, behavioral, cognitive, or well-being outcome. Screen time is the object of measurement agreement study, not linked to an external individual-trait variable as IV or DV.

notes: Study design focuses on measurement agreement and sociodemographic predictors of parent-adolescent discrepancy in screen time reporting. Does not examine associations between screen time and external individual-trait outcomes.
